Dear senior biologist,
 

      This from The Molecular Sciences Institute.  The Institute's mission is to speed the development of genomic technologies and analytical and computational frameworks to enable prediction of the behavior of biological systems.  The reason for the letter is that  Brenner and I are looking for postdocs.  I've attached some project descriptions, but these are examples; we are actually looking for the brightest people and sanguine that appropriate projects will evolve.

       About the Institute.  The place is thriving.  The emerging mix of biologists, computationalists, and physicists is intellectually and sociologically challenging.   Boundaries between labs are blurring; and with luck we will be able to recreate a good deal of the high-independence postdoctoral training environment of the old MRC LMB in fin- de-siecle California.  Relations with UCSF and UC Berkeley are good, and connections  with Berkeley strengths like computer science are deepening nicely.  Although the labs  designed by Ken Kornberg) are beautiful, we still plan to move to the UCSF Mission Bay development in 2002.

      Work here is funded by private contributions, grants to me from NIH/NIGMS, NIH/NHGRI, and Hoechst Marion Roussel; and by a grant to Sydney and me from DARPA for the computation work.  We are on track for further money from US government and philanthropic sources this year.   We thus have both money to pay for research fellows now (those who are not supported by outside fellowships), and the expectation of long-term survival if things continue going well.

    Of course, it may be too early to seriously attempt integrating computation, biology, and  genomic information.  But I don't think so; rather, I believe it's exactly the right time right to try, and that, perched on this interface, if we work well we have a good chance of  producing important science.  At any rate, it is already clear that the culture here, a  borderland where people from very different disciplines are working, is in fact generating original ideas.  And that the continuing importance of genomics to biology allows us to  reasonably anticipate future employment for people who do good science.

    So, although this place is not appropriate for everyone, I hope you would be willing to post this, and encourage those of your students who are highly talented and not averse to risking a sojourn in a new environment to apply.
 

Best regards,
 

Roger Brent
 
 
 
 

Ongoing and contemplated research areas at TMSI
 

Assays for protein interactions in vivo.

Interactions among evolutionarily conserved protein motifs.

Protein based recognition molecules.

Biological and physical probes for cellular events.

Functional genomic instrumentation.

Computational evolutionary biology.

Mathematical frameworks for simulation of cell function.

Directed selection and quantification of fitness in populations of evolving model organisms.

Computational tools to extract functional data from genomic information.
